result:
The first time I watched the sunrise from a moving train somewhere in rural Japan, I understood why humans invented poetry. The sky wasn’t just pink—it was performing. Layers of color folded into each other like silk, and for ten perfect minutes the world felt like it had been designed by someone who actually cared.

I’ve been chasing that feeling ever since.

There’s something quietly rebellious about paying exquisite attention. In a world engineered to fracture our focus, choosing to linger on the way steam curls from a perfect cup of coffee, or how an old man feeds pigeons with devotional seriousness, feels like a small act of civil disobedience. The details are where the miracles hide.

I keep thinking about how every person walking past me on the street is carrying an entire universe inside their skull. Heartbreaks they haven’t told their mothers about. Songs that get them through Tuesdays. Theories about why their cat prefers the left side of the bed. We’re all these walking galaxies pretending to be normal.

Sometimes I write just to prove to myself that I was here. That I noticed. That for one flickering moment I managed to love the world hard enough to try translating it into language, even though I know the translation always fails. The failure itself feels sacred.

The older I get, the more convinced I become that the point was never to become exceptional. The point was to become exquisitely, almost embarrassingly tender toward ordinary things. To let beauty wreck you on a Tuesday afternoon for no good reason. To stay soft in a world that keeps trying to harden you.

(Word count: 257)

In a world engineered to fracture our focus, choosing to linger on the way steam curls from a perfect cup of coffee, or how an old man feeds pigeons with devotional seriousness, feels like a small act of civil disobedience. The details are where the miracles hide.

I keep thinking about how every person walking past me on the street is carrying an entire universe inside their skull. Heartbreaks they haven’t told their mothers about. Songs that get them through Tuesdays. Theories about why their cat prefers the left side of the bed. We’re all these walking galaxies pretending to be normal.

Sometimes I write just to prove to myself that I was here. That I noticed. That for one flickering moment I managed to love the world hard enough to try translating it into language, even though I know the translation always fails. The failure itself feels sacred.

The older I get, the more convinced I become that the point was never to become exceptional. The point was to become exquisitely, almost embarrassingly tender toward ordinary things. To let beauty wreck you on a Tuesday afternoon for no good reason. To stay soft in a world that keeps trying to harden you.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 250 words.
